challenges.title: Just a Logo?
challenges.description:
Ana started writing a document about the Hacknite competition, but all she managed to do was insert an image. Since the document is in PDF format, the position of the image remains fixed regardless of which computer the document is opened on.
Flag format: CTF2022[numbers]

challenges.title: Screenshot
challenges.description:
Shortly after Ivica got his first job, he bragged on social media by posting a screenshot of his welcome email. Of course, he censored the sensitive data.
Flag format: CTF2023[numbers]

challenges.title: Photoshop
challenges.description:
Hana wants to edit images in the future. Unfortunately, she is still a beginner and isn't quite sure how to edit images so they aren't completely black.
Flag format: CTF2022[numbers]

challenges.title: Birds
challenges.description:
Even the birds on the branch—or rather, in this audio recording—know the solution to this task. But are they truly birds, or is it something else entirely?
Flag format: CTF2021[numbers]

challenges.title: Hidden Meaning
challenges.description:
They say a picture is worth a thousand words. It seems this image might just prove that point. Find the solution hidden inside this image.
Flag format: CTF2021[numbers]

challenges.title: The Carriage
challenges.description: The Carriage has returned. Connect using the Linux command nc chal.platforma.hacknite.hr 14011 or the Windows command telnet chal.platforma.hacknite.hr 14011.

Zbh fz7n: T30fhy88 + C3u4p = G2h87vle h 8ls12n e50oc3h.

replies.content: asFfasfasdafasda
Focus heavily on BloodHound and Impacket tools.
The 2026 labs are much more realistic, practice pivoting!
Make sure your reporting is clean, that is where most fail.
Try "http.request" or "ip.addr == x.x.x.x" to start.
tcp.flags.syn == 1 is great for finding port scans.
I usually export objects first to see if there are hidden files.
Ghidra is king for scripting, but IDA UI is still smoother.
Learn both. Some plugins are only available for IDA.
If you are just doing CTFs, Ghidra is more than enough.
Yes, especially when the resolvers directly call raw SQL.
Check out InQL scanner for Burp Suite, it helps a lot.
Always check the input validation on the backend resolvers.
Zsteg is a lifesaver for these kinds of challenges.
Don’t forget to check the strings command first!
Check the metadata with Exiftool before LSB.
Use "pattern_create" from metasploit to find the offset.
Check if DEP/ASLR is disabled on the binary.
mona.py in Immunity Debugger is your best friend here.
Get a Bus Pirate or a Shikra for hardware debugging.
Start with a logic analyzer to see what’s happening.
Multi-meter is essential before you plug anything in.
This is a massive one. Update your browsers immediately!
The exploit uses a JIT optimization bug.
Already seeing some PoCs on Twitter, stay safe.
I am interested! I have won several crypto CTFs before.
Sent you a DM with my Discord handle.
Good luck guys, hope to see you on the leaderboard.
Macros are tricky, try using the "Cookie Jar" settings.
Check out the "PwnFox" extension for easier sessions.
Sometimes Python scripts are faster than Burp Macros.
Frida is the only way to go for modern Android apps.
Look for Objection, it makes Frida much easier to use.
Check the network_security_config.xml file first.
Privileged containers are basically a free root on the host.
Try mounting the /var/run/docker.sock to escape.
Use "deepce" script for automatic Docker enumeration.
Shodan dorks are still very effective for this.
GrayhatWarfare is a great site for searching public buckets.
Be careful, some of those "leaks" are honeypots.
Legacy systems are the only reason it is still alive.
Bleichenbacher attacks are still relevant because of this.
Use OAEP padding if you want to be secure.
Yes, I received an AI voice call last week. It was scary.
Security awareness training needs to be updated ASAP.
Deepfakes are going to destroy trust in digital comms.
Classic MS08-067. Never gets old!
Great writeup, very clear steps for the shellcode generation.
Thanks! I am planning to do Blue next.
Yes, the MT7612U chipset in that Alfa is rock solid for monitor mode.
I prefer the Panda Pau09, it is a bit more stealthy.
Good call, I will check out the Panda as well.
We are moving to Kyber and Dilithium algorithms, so start learning those.
The math behind lattice-based crypto is fascinating but brutal.
RSA-2048 is safe for now, but 4096 is the new minimum for long-term data.
Quantum computers with enough qubits are still years away.
Modern IDSs detect -sS very easily. Try changing the data length with --data-length.
Also, fragmented packets (-f) can sometimes bypass older firewalls.
Don’t forget -T2 to avoid triggering rate-limiting.
2FA is not a silver bullet if you can steal the session cookie.
This is why FIDO2/WebAuthn is the only real protection.
Exactly. Push notifications can be phished, hardware keys cannot.
Evilginx3 setup is a bit tricky with the certificates.
Did you deobfuscate the VBA script yet?
Yes, it leads to a Cobalt Strike beacon.
Can you share the SHA256 hash for Virustotal?
LinPEAS usually catches those instantly.
True, but manual enumeration is a must for OSCP.
I found one recently in a wild crontab entry.
It will automate the boring stuff, but creative exploits still need humans.
I use it to write Python scripts for custom exploits, saves so much time.
Reporting is 50% of the job, AI is a godsend for that.
Just don’t feed sensitive client data into public LLMs!
SAE is much harder because you can’t do offline brute-forcing like WPA2.
You need to be in the middle for the "Dragonblood" attack.
Proxmox is great, but make sure your NIC supports SR-IOV.
I prefer ESXi, but Broadcom is making it hard for home users now.
Try adding a Kali LXC container to save resources.
Interactsh and Burp Collaborator are the best tools for this.
Check out the dnslog.cn if you want a free alternative.
Metasploit is just a tool. A real pro knows how to modify the Ruby scripts.
I prefer writing my own C2, but MSF is great for quick internal pivoting.
Admins still forget to apply the 2020 patches in isolated lab environments.
The FlatProgramAPI in Ghidra is actually very well documented.
Mirai is still alive because of weak Telnet passwords on smart cameras.
SerialKiller is a good library to prevent these kinds of attacks.
Hell’s Gate and Halo’s Gate techniques are a must-read for this.
EDRs are getting smarter with ETW monitoring though.
Chainalysis is too expensive, try using Maltego with crypto transforms.
No more NOP sleds on some ARM architectures due to alignment!
Always verify the "kid" header to prevent path traversal in keys.
Havoc is great because it is open-source and very stealthy.
Proxmark3 is still more powerful than Flipper for HID Prox cards.
The anti-analysis tricks in LockBit 3.0 are insane.
Always use rootless mode if your application allows it.
Rubeus is the gold standard for this on Windows.
Start with HackerOne 101 to learn the basics.
Bugcrowd has better VDPs for beginners.
Requests-html is also great for scraping targets.
The mkfifo shell is the most reliable one for Linux.
Hostapd-mana is perfect for creating these rogue APs.
LibAFL is starting to implement some cool ML features.
Stack pivots in kernel mode are much more restricted now.
Volatility 3 is much faster than the old version.
Gimp is surprisingly good for creating these headers.
Electron apps are famous for this vulnerability.
Check the service account tokens in /var/run/secrets.
Curve25519 is the best balance between speed and security.
SkyHigh is a great tool for scanning open buckets.
I wrote one for detecting Log4j back in the day.
Flashrom is the tool you need for the actual dumping.
Intel471 and Flashpoint do a lot of this heavy lifting.
Its basically a container-based root exploit. Very clean.
Great for bypassing basic User-Agent filters.
Social engineering is 80% prep and 20% execution.
ARM TrustZone is where the real security happens.
SetWindowsHookEx is the API function you want.
Puppeteer + AI is a deadly combo for web automation.
Isolate the infected machine first, then dump the RAM.
The RockYou2024 list is huge but very effective.
Try using the -Me flag for recursive extraction.
Follina (MSDT) was a game changer for Office exploits.
ScyllaHide plugin handles most of these automatically.
Chisel is much better than ProxyChains for stable pivots.
CosmicStrand is a terrifying example of this.
Classic attack. If it is exposed, it is owned.
Discord + CTFd is all you need for coordination.
Subjack can automate the scanning of these records.
DeepSound is the classic tool for this.
Purple Teaming is actually where the most money is.
Bug bounties are safer, but brokers pay 10x more.
Reentrancy is still the #1 cause of lost funds in DeFi.

wiki_articles.title: Introduction to Reverse Engineering
wiki_articles.content: Reverse engineering is the process of analyzing existing software or hardware to understand its functionality, structure, and design. This article explains the basic concepts of reverse engineering, including disassembly, debugging, and binary code analysis. Learn how to use tools like IDA Pro, Ghidra, and Radare2 for program analysis. We will also cover the legal aspects of reverse engineering and how it is applied in security analysis.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Basic concepts of reverse engineering and tools for binary code analysis.

wiki_articles.title: Static vs Dynamic Analysis
wiki_articles.content: There are two main approaches in reverse engineering: static and dynamic analysis. Static analysis involves analyzing code without executing it, while dynamic analysis observes the program during execution. In this article, we explain in detail the advantages and disadvantages of each approach, techniques such as symbolic execution, fuzzing, and tracing. We will also show how to combine these two methods for more efficient analysis.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Comparative analysis of static and dynamic methods in reverse engineering.

wiki_articles.title: Malware Analysis with Ghidra
wiki_articles.content: Ghidra is a free reverse engineering tool developed by the NSA. In this practical guide, we show how to use Ghidra to analyze malicious software. We will cover: loading and analyzing files, identifying malicious functions, code deobfuscation, and IOC indicator extraction. We will also analyze a real malware sample step by step.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Practical guide for malware analysis using Ghidra.

wiki_articles.title: Debugging with x64dbg
wiki_articles.content: x64dbg is a powerful debugger for the Windows platform. This article provides a complete guide to using x64dbg for reverse engineering. We will cover: setting breakpoints, analyzing registers, execution tracing, memory modification, and bypassing protection mechanisms. We will also show how to use scripts and plugins to automate tasks.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Detailed guide for using the x64dbg debugger in reverse engineering.

wiki_articles.title: Code Obfuscation and Deobfuscation Techniques
wiki_articles.content: Obfuscation is a technique used to make code difficult to understand and analyze. In this article, we explore various obfuscation techniques such as: control flow flattening, string encryption, virtual machine obfuscation. We will also show deobfuscation techniques for each of these methods. Special focus is on automatic deobfuscation tools.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Code obfuscation techniques and methods for their deobfuscation.

wiki_articles.title: Buffer Overflow for Beginners
wiki_articles.content: Buffer overflow is one of the oldest and most common software vulnerabilities. This article explains the basics of buffer overflow attacks: how it happens, how to exploit it, and how to fix it. We will cover stack-based buffer overflow, buffer overrun, and return address overwrite. We will demonstrate all concepts on simple C programs.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Basic concepts of buffer overflow vulnerabilities and exploitation.

wiki_articles.title: ROP Chain Exploitation
wiki_articles.content: Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) is a powerful technique that allows execution of arbitrary code in the presence of protections such as DEP and ASLR. In this article, we learn how to construct ROP chains using gadgets from existing code. We will cover: gadget hunting, chain building, stack pivoting, and ROP for different architectures.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Complete guide for constructing ROP chains for exploitation.

wiki_articles.title: Heap Exploitation Techniques
wiki_articles.content: Heap exploitation is a more advanced form of exploitation that targets dynamically allocated memory. In this article, we explore: heap overflow, use-after-free, double free, unlink attacks, and heap spraying. We will also cover protection mechanisms such as heap cookies and safe unlinking.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Advanced techniques for heap memory exploitation.

wiki_articles.title: Format String Vulnerabilities
wiki_articles.content: Format string vulnerabilities occur when user input is directly passed to functions like printf. This article explains how format string attacks work, how they can be used to read and write memory, and how to protect against them. We will cover: reading memory, writing to memory, and GOT overwrite techniques.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Exploitation of format string vulnerabilities for reading and writing memory.

wiki_articles.title: ASLR and DEP Bypass Methods
wiki_articles.content: ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) and DEP (Data Execution Prevention) are modern protections against exploitation. In this article, we learn how to bypass these protections using techniques such as: memory leaks, ROP chains, JIT spraying, and return-to-libc attacks. We will also cover mitigations like CFG.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Methods for bypassing ASLR and DEP protections.

wiki_articles.title: Basic Cryptographic Concepts
wiki_articles.content: Cryptography is the science of protecting information through encryption. This introductory article explains: symmetric and asymmetric encryption, hash functions, digital signatures, and PKI infrastructure. We will cover basic algorithms such as AES, RSA, and SHA.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Introduction to basic concepts and algorithms of cryptography.

wiki_articles.title: AES Encryption in Practice
wiki_articles.content: Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) is the standard for symmetric encryption. In this article, we explain in detail the AES algorithm: SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey transformations. We will also show how to implement AES in Python and analyze its security.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Detailed analysis of the AES algorithm and practical implementation.

wiki_articles.title: RSA and Asymmetric Cryptography
wiki_articles.content: RSA is the first practical asymmetric cryptographic algorithm. This article explains the mathematical foundations of RSA: modular arithmetic, Euler's theorem, public and private key selection. We will also cover attacks on RSA: factoring attacks, timing attacks, and common modulus attack.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Mathematical foundations and security aspects of the RSA algorithm.

wiki_articles.title: Hash Functions and Their Vulnerabilities
wiki_articles.content: Hash functions map arbitrary data to a fixed-length output. In this article, we explore: MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, and SHA-3 hash functions. We will cover vulnerabilities such as: collisions, pre-image attacks, and length extension attacks. We will also cover applications in blockchain technology.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Analysis of hash functions and their security vulnerabilities.

wiki_articles.title: Quantum Cryptography and the Post-Quantum Era
wiki_articles.content: Quantum computers threaten classical cryptography. This article explores: quantum encryption, QKD protocols, and post-quantum cryptographic algorithms. We will cover: lattice-based cryptography, code-based cryptography, and multivariate systems.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Impact of quantum computers on the future of cryptography.

wiki_articles.title: Introduction to Steganography
wiki_articles.content: Steganography is the art and science of hiding information within other data. This introductory article explains basic concepts: LSB method, transformations in the frequency domain, and statistical analysis. We will cover the difference between steganography and cryptography.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Basic principles of hiding information in digital media.

wiki_articles.title: LSB Steganography in Images
wiki_articles.content: Least Significant Bit (LSB) method is the most common approach to steganography. In this article, we learn how to hide messages in images using LSB. We will cover: PNG, BMP, and JPEG formats, hiding capacity, and detection through statistical analysis. We will also show practical implementation in Python.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Detailed guide for LSB steganography in image files.

wiki_articles.title: Audio Steganography Techniques
wiki_articles.content: Audio files offer unique opportunities for steganography. In this article, we explore: LSB in audio files, echo hiding, phase coding, and spread spectrum methods. We will also analyze resistance to compression and statistical detection.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Methods for hiding information in audio recordings.

wiki_articles.title: Steganalysis and Hidden Data Detection
wiki_articles.content: Steganalysis is the science of detecting hidden messages. This article covers: statistical detection methods, histogram analysis, and machine learning for steganography detection. We will also show how to use tools like StegExpose and StegDetect.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Methods for discovering hidden information in digital media.

wiki_articles.title: Advanced Steganography Techniques
wiki_articles.content: Advanced steganography techniques include: transformations in the frequency domain (DCT, DWT), adaptive methods, and steganography in video files. We will also cover steganography in text and network protocols.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Advanced information hiding methods with improved resistance.

wiki_articles.title: Introduction to Web Security
wiki_articles.content: Web security is a critical aspect of modern cyber security. This introductory article explains: OWASP Top 10, HTTP protocol, sessions and cookies, and basic protection mechanisms. We will cover client-side and server-side security.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Basic concepts of web application security.

wiki_articles.title: Complete SQL Injection Guide
wiki_articles.content: SQL Injection is one of the most dangerous web vulnerabilities. In this article, we learn: how SQLi works, different types (Union-based, Error-based, Blind, Time-based), and how to protect against it. We will also show practical examples of exploitation and prevention.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Complete guide to SQL Injection vulnerabilities and countermeasures.

wiki_articles.title: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) Attacks
wiki_articles.content: XSS allows execution of JavaScript code in the victim's browser. This article covers: Reflected XSS, Stored XSS, DOM-based XSS, and their consequences. We will also cover modern protections such as CSP and sanitization.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Comprehensive analysis of XSS vulnerabilities and protection mechanisms.

wiki_articles.title: CSRF and Session Management
wiki_articles.content: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) is an attack that forces a user to perform unwanted actions. In this article, we explain: how CSRF works, session hijacking, and protective mechanisms. We will also cover secure session management and token-based protection.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Analysis of CSRF attacks and secure session management techniques.

wiki_articles.title: Web Application Firewalls and Security Headers
wiki_articles.content: Web Application Firewalls (WAF) and security headers are critical for web application protection. This article covers: WAF configuration, rules, bypass techniques, and security headers like CSP, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options. We will also show how to test WAF effectiveness.
wiki_articles.excerpt: Configuration and testing of WAF and security headers for web protection.

wiki_categories.name: Reverse Engineering
wiki_categories.description: Learn disassembly, debugging, and binary analysis techniques.

wiki_categories.name: Binary Exploitation
wiki_categories.description: Master buffer overflows, ROP chains, and memory corruption.

wiki_categories.name: Cryptography
wiki_categories.description: Understand encryption algorithms, hashing, and cryptanalysis.

wiki_categories.name: Steganography
wiki_categories.description: Discover hidden messages in digital media and files.

wiki_categories.name: Web Security
wiki_categories.description: Explore web vulnerabilities and exploitation techniques.
